coredata.dc:title: High performance fluorescence biosensing of cysteine in human serum with superior specificity based on carbon dots and cobalt-derived recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haifang
Sun, Yuanqiang
Yang, Jie
Hu, Yalei
Yang, Ran
Li, Zhaohui
Qu, Lingbo
Lin, Yuehe
coredata.dc:description: Elevated levels of cysteine (Cys) have been associated with many diseases and it is of vital importance to discriminate and quantify Cys in human serum. However, analogues of Cys especially homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) possess similar structure, which lead to a big challenge for specific detection of Cys without interference from other coexist biothiols. In this work, we developed a novel sensing approach for highly selective and sensitive determination of Cys by using fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) and cobalt-derived specific recognition. To construct such a sensing platform, cobalt-doped CDs (Co-CDs) were synthesized directly by using folic acid (FA) as precursor and CoCl2 as dopant. The fluorescence of the Co-CDs can be specifically and intensively suppressed by the addition of Cys due to the combination with cobalt, while Hcy and GSH cannot induce any obvious fluorescence changes. Besides the robust selectivity, this new developed sensing strategy displayed a good correlation with Cys from 0.1 to 100 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 80 nM. This sensing platform was successfully applied to detect Cys in human serum samples with some advantages including highly selective and sensitive, convenient and rapid, as well as cost-effective, suggesting its great potential to be used in specific Cys analyzing in biological and bioanalytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine detection
High selectivity
Cobalt-derived recognition
Fluorescence carbon dots
Human serum samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and competitive detection of Cys
Feasibility analysis of Co-CDs for selective Cys detection
Fluorescence detection of Cys based on Co-CDs
Possible mechanism investigation of the specific FL response of the Co-CDs to the Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of selective cysteine detection based on Co(II)-doped CDs.

(a) TEM image of Co-CDs. The up inset shows size distribution of the Co-CDs, and the down inset shows crystalline of Co-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of the obtained Co-CDs. (c) High-resolution Co2p XPS spectra of the obtained Co-CDs. (d) UV − vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of Co-CDs, inset shows the photograph of Co-CDs under UV light.

Feasibility analysis of Co-CDs for Cys over GSH/Hcy. FL spectra of (1) Co-CDs, (2) Cys, (3) GSH, (4) Hcy, (5) GSH then Cys, (6) Hcy then Cys. The concentration of Cys/GSH/Hcy is 1 mM, Co-CDs is 40 μg/mL.

a) Fluorescence spectra of Co-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Cys (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μM). (b) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Cys from 0 μM to 1000 μM. Data represents the average of five independent experiments done on different days. Inset: a linear plot was obtained in the concentration range of Cys from 0.1 μM to100 μM. LOD = 80 nM.

Fluorescence responses of Co-CDs toward several interferences. The black bars represent the addition of 1 mM of ascorbic acid (AA), amino acids (Met, Pro, Val, Lys, Ala, Ile, His, Leu, Thr, Arg, Ser, Gly) and the other two biological thiols (Hcy: 0.1 mM, GSH: 1 mM), the concentration of Cys is 0.1 mM. The red bars represent the subsequent addition of 0.1 mM Cys to the above solution. Data represents the average of three independent experiments done on different days (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

a) Fluorescence response of Co-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Cys, MEA, NAC, Hcy, and GSH. Data represents the average of three independent experiments done on different days. b) Chemical structure of Cys, MEA, NAC, Hcy and GSH. c) Schematic recognition of Co-CDs to Cys.
Determination of Cys levels in human serum samples using Co-CDs a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cys and the serum were incubated in 10 mM PBS of pH 7.4 for 10 min. Co-CDs concentration was 0.1 mg/mL in PBS. Human serum samples were subjected to a 50-fold dilution with PBS. The incubation temperature was 37 °C. The slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm. All the data are based on triplicate measurements.

head.ce:title: High performance fluorescence biosensing of cysteine in human serum with superior specificity based on carbon dots and cobalt-derived recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, WA 99164, United States

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Elevated levels of cysteine (Cys) have been associated with many diseases and it is of vital importance to discriminate and quantify Cys in human serum. However, analogues of Cys especially homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) possess similar structure, which lead to a big challenge for specific detection of Cys without interference from other coexist biothiols. In this work, we developed a novel sensing approach for highly selective and sensitive determination of Cys by using fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) and cobalt-derived specific recognition. To construct such a sensing platform, cobalt-doped CDs (Co-CDs) were synthesized directly by using folic acid (FA) as precursor and CoCl2 as dopant. The fluorescence of the Co-CDs can be specifically and intensively suppressed by the addition of Cys due to the combination with cobalt, while Hcy and GSH cannot induce any obvious fluorescence changes. Besides the robust selectivity, this new developed sensing strategy displayed a good correlation with Cys from 0.1 to 100 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 80 nM. This sensing platform was successfully applied to detect Cys in human serum samples with some advantages including highly selective and sensitive, convenient and rapid, as well as cost-effective, suggesting its great potential to be used in specific Cys analyzing in biological and bioanalytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the small molecular biological thiols, cysteine (Cys) plays important roles in human bodies as antioxidant, metal cofactor binder in enzymes, and stabilizer to increase the rigidity of the protein structure by disulfide bond formation [1–3]. Elevated levels of Cys have been associated with neurotoxicity, its deficiency results in many syndromes such as slow growth in children, hair depigmentation, liver damage, muscle and fat loss, and weakness [4,5]. Thus, monitoring the concentration change of Cys is of great importance for diagnosing the related diseases. However, it is important to highlight that Cys, glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcy) have the similar structures and it is still a big challenge to differentiate these three biomolecules from one another. Normally, Hcy concentrations range is 5–12 μM and are implicated in various types of vascular and renal diseases [6,7]. GSH concentrations in human serum range from 7 to 21 μM [8,9], and abnormal level of GSH will lead to liver damage, leucocyte loss, psoriasis, and cancers [10]. Since Cys, Hcy and GSH have similar structures but associated with different diseases, it is of vital importance to distinguish these three biomolecules.

In the past decade, based on the robust and fine properties, nanomaterials have an explosion of interest for the development of efficient molecular diagnostic tools [11–13]. The sensing stategies based on fluorescence nanomaterials provide a promising way for the determination of Cys [14–18]. Among them, fluorescent CDs have attracted significant and growing attention due to their excellent properties including fine chemical and photo-physical properties, low cost, environment friendly, and inexhaustible supplying as well as naturally abundant of precursors [19–21]. Based on the strong affinity of Cys toward metal ions, many CDs-based fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cys have been developed by employing displacement sensing mechanism [22,23]. In such strategies, fluorescence of the synthesized CDs is firstly quenched by addition of metal ions through the formation of CDs-metal complex. In the presence of Cys, the combined metal ions are released from the complex through the chelation with thiol-containing amino acids, thereby leading to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. However, there are some obvious shortcomings for displacement strategy, for example, various toxic heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Au+ and Ag+ were used in the strategy [24–26], and the chelated metal ions could be displaced by other metal ions or thio-containing molecules rather than Cys, which may cause potential interference in practical applications.

Currently, heteroatoms doping has emerged as the major renowned pathway to tune the intrinsic properties of CDs. Different dopant atoms provide CDs with various optical properties [27–30], which might be used as novel sensing platform for the detection of Cys based on metal-doped CDs rather than displacement strategy. Back to 1931 and 1956 [31,32], researchers reported that Co ions have unique and highly specific binding affinity with Cys through the combination of nitrogen of the amino group, sulfur of the sulfhydryl group and Co ions, showing that Co has a great potential to be doped in CDs as a new designed fluorescent probe for Cys detection with satisfied specificity. Recently, Co-doped CDs (Co-CDs) with fine properties have been successfully synthesized by several research groups [33–35]. However, the investigations of Co-CDs are remaining at a very early stage, and their applications on Cys quantification have not been reported. Herein in the present work, we developed a novel sensing platform for specific Cys detection based on Co-CDs. First of all, highly photoluminescent Co-CDs were synthesized by using folic acid (FA) both as the carbon source and nitrogen source, and CoCl2 as dopant (Scheme 1 ). Different with the commonly employed displacement strategy, Co ions were directly doped in CDs by hydrothermal method in this study. In the presence of Cys, the fluorescence of Co-CDs can be specifically and intensively suppressed due to the combination of Cobalt and Cys, while Hcy and GSH cannot induce any obvious fluorescence changes. Furthermore, the obtained Co-CDs were successfully utilized for Cys determination in human serum samples. Therefore, the proposed Co-CDs-based new sensing platform has promising future in specific and sensitive detection of Cys in biological and clinical applications.

FA, Cys, Hcy, GSH, L-Glycine (Gly), L-leucine (Leu), L-isoleucine (Ile), L-lysine (Lys), L-proline (Pro), L-serine (Ser), L-valine (Val), and L-tyrosine (Tyr) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, United States). The metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+) and human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), albumin from chicken egg white (ACEW) were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Histone and D-biotin were purchased from sangon biotech (Shanghai, China). The PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) was used for the sensing of Cys. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water, obtained through a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, USA) having an electric resistance >18.2 MΩ. All reagents were used as received without any further purification.

The synthesis of Co(II)-doped CDs was based on a facial hydrothermal route. Briefly, 5.0 mg (0.011 mM) FA and 5.2 mg (0.022 mM) cobalt chloride were dissolved into 5 mL deionized water with assistance of ultrasonic treatment for about 10 min. The homogeneous mixture was then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and was heated to 190 °C. The reaction was kept for 4 h and then cooled down to room temperature. The product was centrifuged at 10,278g for 10 min to remove any precipitations, followed by being dialyzed against water with a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) = 1000) for 24 h. For comparison with Co-CDs, FA CDs share the same synthetic route as that of Co-CDs but without the addition of cobalt chloride salts.

UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded and then collected on a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) in 1 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence measurements were recorded by an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY) were obtained using the absolute QY measurement system on a FLS 980 fluorometer (Edinburgh Instrument, British) equipped with an integrating sphere (IS), which consists of a 120 mm spherical cavity. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was characterized at room temperature by a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). Morphological details and sizes of Co-CDs were determined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2, USA). The purified Co-CDs solution was placed on a coated copper grid which was left to dry before analysis. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by using an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source.

For Cys detection, 40 μL of Co-CDs (1 mg/mL) were premixed with 950 μL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), and then 10 μL of Cys were added into the mixture. The final volume of each sample was 1 mL, and the final concentrations of Cys was 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μM, respectively. The fluorescent intensity was recorded at room temperature on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer with both the excitation and emission slot widths at 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and competitive detection of Cys
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether the other amino acids and biothiols could interfere with the detection of Cys, the selectivity and competition of the fluorescence sensing assay were further studied. The selectivity was evaluated in the present of other amino acids and biothiols. A fixed concentration of Co-CDs/amino acids was used for the competition step. The final steps were to add 10 μL Cys solution (10 mM) and to measure the emission at 445 nm after 10 min.

The Co-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, FA and Co(II) were heated in DI water at 190 °C for 4 h in an autoclave and then purified using dialysis in water. The TEM image and the size distribution of Co-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. The Co-CDs are nearly uniform and monodisperse with the average size of 3.46 nm (Fig. 1a up inset). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1a down inset) reveals the lattice spacing of the Co-CDs as 0.20 nm, which represents the (100) lattice fringe of graphene structure [36].

Surface status information on Co-CDs was obtained from FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b): OH of 3422 cm−1, NH of 3183 cm−1, CO of 1637 cm−1, CN of 1403 cm−1, and CO of 1303 cm−1. To further confirm the structure information of Co-CDs, XPS measurements were undertaken. As shown in Fig. S1a, the XPS full scan spectrum shows four obvious peaks ascribed to C, N, O, and Co, confirming that the Co is doped in CDs and imparting them suitability for recognition units which can chelate with Cys specifically. The C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1b) exhibits an obvious characteristic peak of CC appearing at 284.8 eV, indicating the abundant carbon structures in Co-CDs. The peaks at 285.7 eV for CN/CO and 288.2 eV for CO confirm the existence of numerous nitrogen and oxygen containing groups. These results indicate that numerous nitrogen containing and oxygen containing groups existed on the Co-CDs surface, which are responsible for high aqueous solubility and stability of Co-CDs. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1c) can be resolved as follows: CNC appearing at 399.5 eV, C − N at 400.4 eV, and NH at 401.5 eV. Moreover, the Co2p spectrum (Fig. 1c) exhibits two predominant peaks at 796.9 and 781.3 eV, which corresponds to the Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2 spin-orbit peaks of CoO, respectively [35,37]. In addition, three satellite peaks observed at 785.8, 788.9 and 802.8 eV are the characteristic peaks of CoO phase [38,39].

The optical properties of Co-CDs were investigated using UV − vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1d, Co-CDs aqueous solution has two obvious UV − vis absorption bands at 273 and 337 nm, which can be attributed to abundant п−п* transition of the aromatic sp2 bond and n−п* transition within Co-CDs. Besides, the Co-CDs dispersion gives a strong FL emission peak at around 445 nm and the Co-CDs aqueous solution illuminated by a 365 nm lamp emits an intense blue luminescence (Fig. 1d inset). The PL emission spectra of Co-CDs exhibit an excitation-dependent feature as depicted in Fig. S2, the emission peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength. At certain excitation wavelength, some corresponding emissive sites are excited and result in the excitation dependent behaviors in emission spectra [40]. As the absorption band at 273 nm, which corresponding to the п−п* transition of the aromatic sp2 band, meaning trap of excited state energy by the carbon core leads to the obvious FL signal. The other absorption band at 337 nm owing to the n−п* transition, the trapping of excited state energy by the surface stated also results in strong FL emission. Fig. S3 shows that the solution of CDs is getting clear and exhibits bright blue fluorescence. FLQYs of Co-CDs were measured by an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield spectrometer, and the result is 44.6%. The FL lifetime of the obtained Co-CDs was measured by time-resolved photoluminescence measurements. As shown in Fig. S5, the observed FL lifetime is 15.16 ns. As shown in Fig. S6a, the normalized PL almost does not change in pH range from 4 to 12. The Co-CDs exhibit excellent photostability which is shown in Fig. S6b, and we can see that the normalized PL of Co-CDs does not change significantly under continuous irradiation. Moreover, the Co-CDs can keep stable when stored at 4 °C for half a month (Fig. S6c). All the results demonstrate that the as-prepared Co-CDs has superior optical properties and stability, which has a great potential to be used as robust fluorescence sensing probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility analysis of Co-CDs for selective Cys detection
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the feasibility of Co-CDs for Cys detection, the fluorescence response of Co-CDs towards Cys was tested in PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4). As shown in Fig. 2 , Co-CDs display strong emission at 445 nm when excited at 273 nm. The fluorescence of Co-CDs decreases significantly upon the addition of Cys (1 mM). In contrast, fluorescence responses do not change for Hcy and GSH (1 mM). These results showed the potential capability of Co-CDs to detect Cys with high selectivity. At the same time, the competition experiments shown that the addition of Hcy and GSH did not lead to visible variation of FL intensities of Co-CDs + Cys (Fig. 2). As shown in Fig. S4, the FL intensity of Co-CDs after addition of Cys is decreased obviously within 5 min. In the case of Hcy, almost no FL signal changes can be observed. The reaction of Cys or Hcy toward Co-CDs has kinetics difference, [15] which results in the specific recognition of Cys based on Co-CDs. The results clearly demonstrated that the biothiols such as Hcy and GSH cannot produce the false signal and cannot affect the interaction between Co-CDs and Cys, suggesting high selectivity of this sensing platform toward Cys detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cys based on Co-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S7a, the Co-CDs exhibit an obvious response towards Cys in pH 6-12. The result indicates that Co-CDs can work over a wide pH range and the best performance is at physiological pH, holding a promise for practical biological applications.

Kinetic study: Time-dependent fluorescence spectra of Co-CDs in the prescence of Cys were monitored. The fluorescence intensity of the obtained Co-CDs rapidly decreased while the Cys was added, and kept stable during the following 30 min (Fig. S7b). Especially, the Co-CDs intensity depressed obviously within 5 min and remained unchanged after 10 min, suggesting a high reaction speed. For Cys detection, the incubation time of 10 min was selected for the following experiments.

Under the optimized conditions discussed above, the linear response range of the sensing system was measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence quenching efficiency increases gradually as the Cys increases from 0 to 1 mM, and the digital photograph was caught out in Fig. S8. These results revealed an excellent exponential correlation between quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and Cys concentrations, where F0 and F refers to the fluorescent intensity of Co-CDs system in the absence and presence of Cys, respectively. The calibration curve between quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and Cys concentrations can be expressed as (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0058c + 0.0235 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9977. Based on the 3σ/slope rule, the detection limit for Cys was calculated to be 80 nM, which is much lower than Cys level in human serum (30–200 μM) [4]. The comparison about the analytical performance between this method and the reported assays was summarized in Table S1. The result demonstrated that our proposed assay exhibited high selectivity, wide linear range and low detection limit, showing a great promise for high performance detection of Cys.

To evaluate the selectivity of the Co-CDs toward Cys, fluorescent response to a range of biothiols and amino acids were investigated including competitive biothiols especially GSH and Hcy. As shown in Fig. 4 , a significant decrease for fluorescence intensity of the Co-CDs is observed upon the addition of Cys (100 μM). In sharp contrast to Cys, fluorescence response does not change for amino acids (Met, Pro, Val, Lys, Ala, Ile, His, Leu, Thr, Arg, Ser, Gly) and also for the other two biological thiols (Hcy, GSH), even when GSH was higher about millimolar level. Competition experiments were also carried out by adding 1 mM of ascorbic acid (AA), other two biological thiols (Cys, GSH) and other amino acids to 100 μM Cys solutions. The fluorescence responses of the Co-CDs were shown in Fig. 4. Co-CDs show almost unchanged FL-quenching effect in the presence of those competing biological thiols and amino acids. The FL stability of Co-CDs toward common cations (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+) and proteins (HSA, BSA, ACEW, histone, D-biotin) was recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. S9, there is no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity for CDs. All these results clearly indicated that this Co-CDs based sensing platform exhibit an unexpected selectivity and reliability toward Cys and has a great potential to be used in complex biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism investigation of the specific FL response of the Co-CDs to the Cys
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effect of cobalt doping on the performance of this system, CDs without cobalt doping were synthesized as control by using FA as the single precursor. Fluorescence spectra of CDs upon addition of Cys were investigated. It was found that Cys caused almost no quenching of the CDs (Fig. S10). This result reveals that Co is the key factor to identify Cys. First, the UV–vis spectrum of Cys and the fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of Co-CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. S11a, the emission spectrum of Co-CDs is scarcely overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Cys, indicating that FRET is not the fluorescence quenching mechanism of this system. At the same time, there is no clear overlap occurred between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cys and the excitation spectrum of fluorescent Co-CDs, as demonstrated in Fig. S7a, which indicates that the Cys-induced fluorescence quenching of Co-CDs is not consistent with the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. To further reveal the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the corresponding fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of Cys were investigated. As shown in Fig. S11b, there are no obvious changes of the fluorescence lifetime (15.16 ns and 15.37 ns) of the Co-CDs with or without addition of Cys, which implied that the fluorescence quenching of Co-CDs by Cys can be attributed to the SQE mechanism [41]. To gain an insight into the Co-derived recognition, interaction of four Cys analogues, Hcy, GSH, 2-aminoethanethiol (MEA) and N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) with Co-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, NAC causes negligible quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0), while MEA causes identical fluorescence quenching efficiency of Co-CDs as Cys (molecular structures of Cys, MEA and NAC have been shown in Fig. 5b). These results indicated that Co in Co-CDs intends to chelate with −NH2 and −SH of Cys and MEA rather than −SH and COOH of NAC. The strong absorption bands at 273 nm and 337 nm (Fig. S12) implies that Co reacted with Cys and formed a chelate complex on the surface of Co-CDs. The FL quenching might be related to −NH2 and −SH of Cys showing marked tendency to combine with Co and forming a favorable five-membered ring, which is well complied with the reported studies [31,32]. To demonstrate the special role played by cobalt in this sensing system, Se doped CDs were also synthesized as control and the result shows that the FL intensity of Se-CDs is quite stable after the addition of Cys (Fig. S13).

Holding the good properties of outstanding selectivity and high sensitivity of the proposed sensing system, the application of the assay for Cys detection in human serum was evaluated. The serum from volunteer was collected by the first affiliated hospital of Zhengzhou University and informed consent was obtained for the use of human serum. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines and approved by Life-Science Ethics Review Committee of Zhengzhou University. Due to high concentration of Cys in human serum, the human serum was diluted so that Cys concentration could fall in the standard calibration curve of this assay, which also showed that this assay has sufficient sensitivity and only require small amount of real samples. Consequently, the recovery test was studied by spiking different amounts of Cys into the diluted serum samples. As listed in Table 1 , it is found that the Cys concentration in 50-fold diluted human serum samples was determined as 1.931 μM and 2.334 μM, respectively, which is consistent with the reported level [4]. Furthermore, the recoveries of Cys in the spiked samples ranges from 98.3% to 103.2% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 7%. All the results demonstrate that this proposed Co-CDs based Cys sensing platform has great potential to be used in practical applications.

In summary, highly photoluminescent Co-CDs were synthesized by one pot hydrothermal-assisted approach using FA and CoCl2 as precursor. The doped Co(II) remains their high binding affinity towards Cys, and based on which a novel design of biosensing platform for Cys detection was successfully fabricated. Different with the traditional reported displacement strategy, the prepared Co-CDs can be used directly as highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for rapid and convenient quantification of Cys without any interference even in the presence of millimolar range of Hcy and GSH. Furhermore, this low-cost Co-CDs based assay has been applied in the detection of Cys in human serum samples with satisfactory results. It is highly desirable that the idea of metal ion-doping for specific target detection can be widely employed for the design of other metal ions-doped nanomaterials, which has great possibilities to be used as high performance nanoprobes in biosensing studies and bioapplications.
The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21708035, 21605038), the Foundation for University Key Teacher by Henan Province (2017GGJS007), the Outstanding Young Talent Research Fund of Zhengzhou University (1421316038), and the Key Scientific Research Project in Universities of Henan Province (19A150048).

An advanced nitrogen-doped graphene/cobalt-embedded porous carbon polyhedron hybrid for efficient catalysis of oxygen reduction and water splitting
Rapid detection of Cr (VI) ions based on cobalt (II)-doped carbon dots
Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and inherent folate receptor-positive cancer cell targetability
Reduction of bromate by cobalt-impregnated biochar fabricated via pyrolysis of lignin using CO2 as a reaction medium
Cobalt-doped graphitic carbon nitride photocatalysts with high activity for hydrogen evolution
Activating cobalt nanoparticles via the mott-schottky effect in nitrogen-rich carbon shells for base-free aerobic oxidation of alcohols to esters
Multi-doped carbon dots with ratiometric pH sensing properties for monitoring enzyme catalytic reactions
One-pot synthesis of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for sensitive and selective determination of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous solution

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haifang Liu received her Master degree in College of Life Science from Henan Agricultural University. She has been studying for her Ph.D. degree in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University since 2015, where her main research interests are nanomaterial-based Chemo/Biosensors.

Yuanqiang Sun received his Ph.D. degree in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Shanxi University in 2016. He is now an assistant professor at Zhengzhou University and his research interests mainly focus on synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.
Jie Yang is a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University, where her main research interests are nanomaterial-based Chemo/Biosensors.

Yalei Hu is a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University, where his main research interests are nanomaterial synthesis and their applications in Chemo/Biosensing.

Ran Yang received her Ph.D. degree from China Pharmaceutical University in 2006. At present, she is an associate professor of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University. Her research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial-based Chemo/Biosensor.

Zhaohui Li received his Ph.D. degree in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Bio Sensing and Chemometrics at Hunan University in 2007. He did his postdoctoral training in Walt Group at Tufts University from 2007 to 2009 and in Pacific Northwest National Laboratory from 2009 to 2011. At present, he is a full professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial preparation and their applications in Chemo/Biosensing.

Lingbo Qu received his Ph.D. degree from China Pharmaceutical University in 1997. He did his postdoctoral training in pharmaceutical institute at Peking Union Medical College from 1997 to 1999. At present, he is a full professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on drug molecule design, drug analysis and nanomaterial-based Chemo/Biosensing.

Yuehe Lin is a full professor at Washington State University and a Laboratory Fellow at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. His research interests include electrochemistry, bioanalytical chemistry, chemical sensors and biosensors, fuel cells and batteries, material synthesis and their applications.
